sets.passage: The summer I turned fifteen, I decided to start my own small (1)___: selling homemade lemonade at the local market. I imagined easy money and instant (2)___. Reality, of course, had other plans. On my first morning, I had badly misjudged the (3)___, mixing the lemonade far too sour, and my few customers walked away with (4)___ faces. I had also forgotten that people needed (5)___, and lost several sales because I had nothing to give them their drinks in. By noon, I had sold almost (6)___ and was ready to give up. But my grandmother, who had lent me the money, encouraged me to (7)___ from each mistake instead of quitting. So the next day, I adjusted my (8)___, brought proper cups, and added a cheerful sign. Sales slowly began to (9)___. Over the following weeks, I learned to listen to my customers, to (10)___ my prices fairly, and to keep going on slow days. By the end of the summer, my little stall was actually making a small (11)___. The money was modest, but what I had truly gained was far more (12)___. I had learned how a business really works, how to recover from (13)___, and how much patience real success demands. Most of all, I learned that almost everyone who (14)___ in the end has first failed, often many times. The secret was not avoiding mistakes, but refusing to let them (15)___ me.

sets.passage: There was a boy in our class named Sam whom others liked to (1)___. He was quiet and a little different, and a small group seemed to enjoy making his life (2)___. Like most of the class, I simply looked (5)___ when the teasing began. But one day, I saw Sam sitting alone at lunch, his eyes red, trying hard not to (6)___, and something in me finally (7)___. I realised that my silence was not neutral; by saying nothing, I was quietly taking the bullies' (8)___. So, with my heart pounding, I walked over and simply sat down (9)___ him. It was such a small act, but it felt enormous. The next day I did it again, and soon one or two others (10)___ us. Slowly, Sam was no longer (11)___. The bullies, robbed of an audience, gradually lost (12)___. Sam and I became real friends, and he later told me that my sitting with him that day had meant more than I could ever (13)___. I learned that the opposite of bullying is not always grand (14)___; often it is just ordinary people refusing to stay silent. Looking away is easy, but it makes us part of the problem. Sometimes all it takes to change everything is the courage to (15)___ beside someone who has been left alone.

sets.passage: For two years, I carried a heavy (1)___ against my closest friend, Leo. He had betrayed a secret I had trusted him with, and the hurt had turned into a cold, hard (2)___. We stopped speaking. Whenever his name came up, I felt the old (3)___ rise again. I told myself I was right to stay (4)___, that he did not deserve my forgiveness. And perhaps he did not. But what I slowly came to realise was that my anger was hurting (5)___ far more than it was hurting him. I carried it everywhere, a constant weight on my (6)___. It poisoned my happy moments and disturbed my (7)___ at night. One day, exhausted by it, I understood that forgiving Leo was not really about (8)___ what he had done, or even about him at all. It was about setting (9)___ free. So I wrote to him. I did not pretend the hurt had not been (10)___, but I told him I no longer wished to carry the anger. To my surprise, he replied with a long, genuine (11)___. We did not instantly become best friends again, and some trust, once broken, is hard to fully (12)___. But the moment I let go of the resentment, I felt as if a great stone had been (13)___ from my chest. I learned that forgiveness is not a gift we give to those who hurt us; far more often, it is a gift we give (14)___. To forgive is simply to stop letting the past (15)___ the present.

sets.passage: Every year on a cold March morning, our whole town gathers on the bare hillside above the river to plant (1)___. The tradition began twenty years ago, after a terrible flood, when people realised that the bare slopes could no longer hold the (2)___. So they decided to act. The first year, only a few dozen people came, and the tiny saplings looked (3)___ on the vast, empty hill. Many doubted the effort would make any (4)___. But the volunteers returned, year after year, each spring planting a few thousand more (5)___ trees. Slowly, the hillside began to (6)___. What had been bare brown earth turned, season by season, into a spreading green (7)___. The roots gripped the soil, and when the heavy rains came, the land no longer washed (8)___ as it once had. Birds and insects returned to the new woodland, and the river below ran (9)___ than it had in living memory. Today, walking beneath those tall trees, it is hard to believe the hill was once (10)___. The forest stands as living proof of what a community can (11)___ when it works together patiently over many (12)___. None of the original planters will ever see the woodland reach its full (13)___; many have already passed away. Yet they planted anyway, for children they would never (14)___. sets.passage: When our old dog, Max, fell seriously (1)___, the vet gently warned us that his recovery would be long and far from (2)___. For weeks, Max could barely walk, and caring for him became a daily (3)___. He needed medicine four times a day, help to stand, and constant (4)___. It was exhausting, and there were nights when, watching him struggle, I secretly (5)___ whether we were doing the right thing. But Max had been part of our family for twelve years, through every joy and (6)___, and we could not simply give up on (7)___. So we took turns sitting with him, hand-feeding him when he would not (8)___, and carrying him gently into the garden. Progress was painfully (9)___. Some days he seemed a little stronger; others, he slipped (10)___ again, and our hearts sank. We learned to celebrate the smallest (11)___ — a wagging tail, a few steps taken alone. Slowly, against the vet's careful expectations, Max began to (12)___. Months later, he was trotting around the garden again, grey-faced but (13)___. Those difficult weeks taught me what love really (14)___.
